paperTitle: Singapore-Cambridge GCE O-Level 1128 English Language — AI-authored Practice Paper 27: The Old Radio (Paper 2 §B style)

sections.instruction: Read the narrative text below about a teenager who, after his grandfather's death, switches on the old radio that once filled their flat with Mandarin oldies and Rediffusion broadcasts, and answer the questions that follow. Section B [14 marks] — AI-authored practice paper modelled on the Singapore-Cambridge 1128/02 Section B format. Original passage written for the morning quiz bank; not from a past exam paper. 10 short-answer items (Q1–Q10) on language use and inference, plus a 4-item flowchart MCQ (Q11) on the narrator's emotion arc, are included.
sections.passage:
Paragraph 1
The radio had sat on the same corner of the kitchen counter for as long as I could remember — a squat brown box with a cracked dial and a single silver knob worn smooth by my grandfather's thumb. Every morning before the rest of the flat stirred, he would lean over it, turn the knob a hair's breadth at a time, and coax a station out of the static. By the time the rice was steaming, the kitchen of our three-room flat in Toa Payoh would be full of Mandarin oldies — Teresa Teng, Wang Chiang Sang, voices older than my mother.

Paragraph 2
Grandfather called it his 'Rediffusion box', though it had not picked up Rediffusion since long before I was born. He said it the way other people said the name of an old friend. When I was small I thought the singers lived inside it, and that Grandfather, by turning the knob just so, was politely asking them to come out and sing for us. He never corrected me. 'Listen,' he would only say, tapping the counter in time. 'This one your grandmother liked.'

Paragraph 3
As I grew older the radio became background, then nuisance. By Secondary Three I had earphones of my own, a phone full of songs no one in the flat had heard of. The brown box and its crackling oldies belonged to a slower world, and I had places to be. I do not remember the last morning I sat and listened with him. That is the cruelty of last times — they pass without announcing themselves.

Paragraph 4
Grandfather went into hospital in March and did not come home. For two weeks after the funeral the radio stayed dark. No one switched it on. No one moved it either; it would have felt like moving him. The kitchen was very quiet in the mornings, and I had not known, until then, how much of the silence he used to fill.

Paragraph 5
One Saturday, when the flat was empty, I found myself standing in front of it. My thumb fitted the worn groove in the knob exactly. I turned it the way I had watched him turn it ten thousand times — a hair's breadth, then another — and the static parted, and a woman's voice swam up out of the years, singing something slow in a dialect I half understood.

Paragraph 6
The sound went straight through me. For one disorienting second the kitchen smelled of steaming rice and his menthol balm, and he was there, leaning at my shoulder, tapping the counter in time. Then the second passed and he was not there, and the gap where he had been was the loudest thing in the room.

Paragraph 7
I almost switched it off. My finger was on the knob. But I did not. I sat down on his stool instead, the way he used to, and let the song finish. It was a sad song and I did not understand all of it, but I understood that he had loved it, and that was enough to keep me sitting there. When it ended the announcer read out a dedication from a listener in Ang Mo Kio to her late husband, and I thought: so I am not the only one who comes here to find someone.

Paragraph 8
Now I switch it on most mornings before school. The songs are still older than my mother and I still catch only half the words. My friends would not understand it and I have stopped trying to explain. The radio is not Grandfather, and turning the knob does not bring him back. But for the length of one slow song, in a kitchen that smells faintly of rice, I get to keep a little company with the man who taught me how to listen.

(AI-authored original narrative for O-Level 1128 §B style practice; not from a past examination paper. Rediffusion: a wired cable-radio service once popular in Singapore homes, famous for its Mandarin and dialect programmes; many older residents still call any old radio set a 'Rediffusion box'. Mandarin oldies: classic Chinese popular songs from the 1960s–1980s, such as those of singer Teresa Teng.)

questions.stem: Q1. In Paragraph 1, what details show that the radio had been used by the grandfather for a very long time? [1]
questions.answer: the dial was cracked and the single silver knob had been 'worn smooth by his thumb' from years of daily use; he had also turned it on every morning for as long as the narrator could remember

questions.stem: Q2. In Paragraph 2, the grandfather said 'Rediffusion box' 'the way other people said the name of an old friend'. What does this comparison suggest about his feelings towards the radio? [1]
questions.answer: it suggests that he felt deep affection and familiarity towards the radio, treating it not as a mere object but as a cherished companion he had known for years

questions.stem: Q3. In Paragraph 2, why did the grandfather never correct the young narrator's belief that the singers 'lived inside' the radio? [1]
questions.answer: he was happy to let the child keep his charming, imaginative idea / he enjoyed sharing that sense of wonder with his grandson and did not want to spoil it with a dull factual explanation

questions.stem: Q4. What is the effect of the phrase 'the radio became background, then nuisance' (Paragraph 3)? [1]
questions.answer: the two-stage shift compresses the narrator's growing indifference into a few words — it traces how the radio went from something he simply stopped noticing to something that actively annoyed him, showing how he drifted away from his grandfather's world as he grew up

questions.stem: Q5. In Paragraph 3, what does the narrator mean by 'the cruelty of last times — they pass without announcing themselves'? [1]
questions.answer: he means that we never know at the time that a particular moment will be the last one of its kind, so we fail to treasure it; he never realised the last morning he listened with his grandfather was the last, and so lost the chance to value it

questions.stem: Q6. In Paragraph 4, why does the narrator say that switching on or moving the radio 'would have felt like moving him'? [1]
questions.answer: the radio had become so closely bound up with the grandfather's presence that disturbing it felt like disturbing the grandfather himself / touching the radio would mean accepting that he was truly gone, which the family was not yet ready to do

questions.stem: Q7. What is the effect of the description in Paragraph 5: 'a woman's voice swam up out of the years'? [1]
questions.answer: the word 'swam' makes the voice seem to rise slowly and effortlessly through time, as if surfacing from deep memory; the phrase 'out of the years' suggests the song is bringing the distant past back into the present, heightening the sense that the music carries his grandfather's era with it

questions.stem: Q8. In Paragraph 6, why does the narrator call the gap where his grandfather had been 'the loudest thing in the room'? [1]
questions.answer: it is a paradox — an absence cannot literally make a sound, yet his grandfather's absence felt so overwhelming and impossible to ignore that it dominated the narrator's awareness more than any actual noise could, emphasising the depth of his loss

questions.stem: Q9. In Paragraph 7, what made the narrator decide not to switch the radio off, even though his finger was already on the knob? [1]
questions.answer: he realised that, although he could not fully understand the song, his grandfather had loved it, and sharing in something his grandfather had loved felt like a way of staying close to him — that connection was enough to make him stay and let the song finish

questions.stem: Q10. In Paragraph 8, what does the narrator mean by the closing idea that he gets 'to keep a little company with the man who taught me how to listen'? [1]
questions.answer: playing the radio is no longer about the songs themselves but about feeling near to his grandfather; 'taught me how to listen' suggests his grandfather passed on not just a love of music but a way of paying attention and valuing quiet moments — so the morning ritual lets him remember and honour that gift while accepting that his grandfather is gone

sections.instruction: Q11. The narrator's dominant feeling shifts as the passage moves from memories of his grandfather to switching on the radio after the loss. For each part of the passage, choose ONE word from the bank that best describes his dominant feeling at that moment. There are some extra words in the bank you do not need to use. Word bank: fondness / grief / overwhelmed / comforted / proud / impatient / amused / resentful. The original task carries 4 marks; each blank is split into its own MCQ so each sub-part can be auto-graded independently.
sections.passage: Refer to the same 'Old Radio' narrative in Exercise 1 above. The narrator's feelings shift across the passage — from fondness as he recalls his grandfather coaxing Mandarin oldies out of the static each morning, to grief in the quiet weeks after the funeral when the radio stays dark and the kitchen is silent, to overwhelmed the moment the song swims up and the kitchen briefly smells of rice and menthol balm with the gap where his grandfather had been the loudest thing in the room, ending comforted as the morning ritual becomes a gentle way of keeping company with the man who taught him to listen. Pick the single best word from the bank for each moment.

questions.stem: Q11(i). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 1-2, as he recalls his grandfather leaning over the radio each morning, coaxing out Mandarin oldies and saying 'this one your grandmother liked'? [1]
Q11(ii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 4, in the two weeks after the funeral when the radio stays dark and the kitchen is very quiet in the mornings? [1]

Q11(iii). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraphs 5-6, when the song swims up out of the static, the kitchen briefly smells of rice and menthol balm, and the gap where his grandfather had been is the loudest thing in the room? [1]

Q11(iv). What best describes the narrator's dominant feeling in Paragraph 8, now that he switches the radio on most mornings and gets to keep a little company with the man who taught him how to listen? [1]
